Langenthal

BEBern

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The linen weavers' secret

A child discovers a hidden loom in a Marktgasse cellar that weaves stories into cloth. Each fabric tells the tale of a different family from 1640s Langenthal, when linen sailed across Europe to Spain and Portugal. To save the last thread from breaking, the child must trace the cloth's journey from the weaver's workshop to a merchant ship on the Mediterranean.

Create This Story

From 1640, Langenthal became a centre of linen canvas production, exporting to France, Italy, Spain, and Portugal. In 1704, the town became the seat of the Oberaargau Canvas Dealers' Guild, uniting weavers, shopkeepers, and traders. The Marktgasse was paved in 1730 and became the commercial spine of the town. By 1756, the linen trade had brought such prosperity that Langenthal had grown to 189 households, three taverns, and a new warehouse built in 1748.

The last plate from the Porzi

A porcelain plate from the Langenthal factory refuses to break, no matter how many times it falls. A child follows its journey backwards in time -- from a grandmother's Sunday table in the 1960s, through the hands of 950 factory workers, to a Bohemian clay pit where the kaolin was first dug -- discovering why this one plate carries the memory of an entire nation's kitchen.

Create This Story

The Porzellanfabrik Langenthal was founded on 4 July 1906 by entrepreneur Arnold Spychiger with 500,000 Swiss francs in capital. Switzerland had no kaolin deposits, so raw materials were imported from the Karlsbad region (now Czechia), and 35 of the initial 87 workers were recruited from Bohemia. The factory's peak years were 1950-1970, employing up to 950 workers in 1964. For decades, 'Langenthal' porcelain was found in virtually every Swiss household, hotel, and restaurant. Porcelain production ceased in 1998 due to foreign competition.

The Langete's revenge

Before the relief tunnel was built, the Langete floods the streets of Langenthal once more. A child must navigate the canal-like elevated pavements in a tiny boat, racing to warn the town before the water rises higher than the 1975 flood -- discovering along the way why Langenthal's streets were designed like rivers, and that sometimes a town must learn to live with its wildest neighbour.

Create This Story

The Langete river gave Langenthal its name but was also its greatest natural threat. Before modern flood control, the inner-city streets with their elevated pavements acted as overflow channels, flooding on average twice yearly. The catastrophic floods of 1975 caused property damage of at least 60 million Swiss francs. In response, a 7,531-metre relief tunnel was built between 1988 and 1991, capable of diverting up to 58 cubic metres per second from below Madiswil directly into the Aare at Bannwil.

The monks' meadow magic

A child meets a Cistercian monk from St. Urban's Abbey in the 13th century who is building the Waessermatten irrigation system. Together they channel water through meadows and learn to read the landscape like a living map -- discovering that patience and careful planning can transform a flood-prone valley into one of Switzerland's most fertile and beautiful landscapes.

Create This Story

In 1194, the Freiherr of Langenstein founded St. Urban's Abbey, a Cistercian monastery that brought agricultural innovation to the Oberaargau. The monks constructed the elaborate Waessermatten irrigation system of ditches and channels throughout the Langete valley, dating back to the 13th century. This ingenious meadow irrigation system has been practised for roughly a thousand years and is recognized as a nationally significant cultural landscape. The surrounding Oberaargau features rolling hills and the lush water meadows created by the monks' work.

The Choufhuesi clock strikes thirteen

When the clock tower of the 1808 Choufhuesi strikes an impossible thirteenth hour, doors throughout the building open to its past lives. A child wanders through a bustling cloth market, a candlelit prison cell, a dusty library, and finally a modern art gallery where the paintings have come alive -- each room revealing a different century of Langenthal's civic heart.

Create This Story

The Choufhuesi (Kaufhaus) in Langenthal was originally built in 1613 as a market hall, then reconstructed between 1790 and 1808 by architect Joseph Purtschert as a striking late-Baroque building with a distinctive clock tower. It contained a market hall, restaurant, cloth hall, guard room, meeting rooms, a prison, and a library -- a true civic multipurpose building. Since 1992, it has housed the Kunsthaus Langenthal, which presents up to eight temporary exhibitions of contemporary art per year.

The ice hockey ghost of Schoren

On a foggy night at the Eishalle Schoren, a young player sees the ghost of a championship team skating on the ice under the roofed arena. The ghost wears the jersey of the 2012 title-winning squad and has a message for the child: winning is not about the league you play in, but about the pride a town carries in its heart.

Create This Story

SC Langenthal was founded in 1946 and plays at the Eishalle Schoren, built in 1961 and roofed in 1980, with a capacity of approximately 4,300 spectators. The club won three Swiss League championships in 2012, 2017, and 2019, becoming one of the most successful second-tier clubs in Swiss hockey. Following the 2022-23 season, the club voluntarily withdrew from professional hockey due to financial constraints and now competes in the MyHockey League (third tier). Ice hockey remains deeply embedded in Langenthal's identity.

The train that connected everything

In 1857, the first train arrives in Langenthal with a great cloud of steam and a shriek of the whistle. A child climbs aboard the locomotive and rides through the decades, watching from the window as the quiet market town transforms into a bustling industrial centre -- meeting linen weavers, porcelain painters, and machine builders at every stop along the way.

Create This Story

The Swiss Central Railway reached Langenthal in 1857 on the Olten-Bern line, connecting the town to the main east-west axis across the Swiss Plateau. The station became a true junction with additional lines: the Langenthal-Huttwil line to the Emmental, the metre-gauge Langenthal-Oensingen line (opened 26 October 1907), and the Langenthal-Melchnau line (opened 6 October 1917). These railways transformed Langenthal from a quiet market town into an industrial hub, enabling the growth of textile factories, the porcelain works, and machine manufacturers.

The bear on the Gruenenberg

A child explores the ruins of Gruenenberg Castle and awakens a medieval knight who tells the tale of the three castles that once ruled the Oberaargau. As they walk from Gruenenberg to Schnabelburg to Langenstein, the knight grows older with each castle, until they reach St. Urban's Abbey -- where the monks who quietly outlasted them all offer the knight a final bowl of soup and a place to rest.

Create This Story

In the High Middle Ages (12th-15th centuries), three castles dominated the Oberaargau: Gruenenberg, Schnabelburg, and Langenstein. In 1194, the Freiherr of Langenstein founded St. Urban's Abbey, a Cistercian monastery. After the extinction of the Langenstein family in 1212, the abbey inherited additional lands. The lords of Gruenenberg rose to prominence, and in 1279 the abbey was forced to cede the low court and a fortified house in Langenthal to the Freiherr of Gruenenberg as a fief. The monks quietly outlasted all three noble families.
